title: Levetiracetam for blood-spinal cord barrier protection may translate to blood-brain barrier preservation in HIE

input.description: Levetiracetam at 1000-1500 mg/d doses significantly reduces blood-spinal cord barrier permeability in acute spinal cord injury models by suppressing perforin expression and inflammatory cascades. Given that HIE involves blood-brain barrier disruption as a key pathological mechanism, and levetiracetam is already used in pediatric epilepsy with established safety profiles, repurposing levetiracetam for its barrier-protective properties (beyond seizure control) warrants investigation. The drug's anti-inflammatory effects via perforin suppression may provide neuroprotection during the critical 0-2 year neuroplasticity window. Trim47's mechanism of BBB protection through ZO1 interaction suggests tight junction proteins are druggable targets that levetiracetam may indirectly support.

HIE explicitly involves BBB disruption per the retrieval context. Levetiracetam is already used in pediatric populations for seizure control, providing safety data. The mechanistic overlap between barrier protection in spinal cord injury and HIE-related BBB dysfunction suggests translational potential, particularly since Aleksandra may already be on antiseizure medications.", "discovery_method": "Cross-disease inference from spinal cord injury barrier protection to HIE blood-brain barrier pathology, leveraging existing pediatric levetiracetam safety data", "supporting_source_ids": ["pubmed/42088398", "pubmed/42098397"]}
